name: Hawthorn Berry Extract
historicalUse: Hawthorn has been used in Western botanical medicine since at least the first century CE. Dioscorides and Galen described its cardiac applications. In Arabic medicine, Ibn Sina and Ibn al-Baytar documented its use for palpitations and cardiac weakness. European monastic medicine relied heavily on hawthorn as a heart tonic. The German Commission E and European Scientific Cooperative on Phytotherapy (ESCOP) have formally recognized hawthorn for cardiac insufficiency.
classicalSources: Ibn al-Baytar — Comprehensive Book on Simple Medicaments
modernEvidence: Hawthorn is among the best-evidenced cardiac botanical agents. The SPICE and WS 1442 trials demonstrated significant improvements in cardiac output, exercise tolerance, and symptoms in mild-to-moderate heart failure. Active constituents (OPCs, vitexin-rhamnoside, hyperoside) increase coronary blood flow, inhibit ACE and phosphodiesterase, and have positive inotropic effects.
mechanisms: Positive inotrope — increases myocardial contractility via phosphodiesterase inhibition
ACE inhibition — reduces angiotensin II formation, supporting vascular tone
Coronary vasodilation — relaxes smooth muscle, increases coronary blood flow
Antioxidant — OPCs protect vascular endothelium from oxidative damage
Anti-arrhythmic — prolongs refractory period, reduces susceptibility to ventricular arrhythmia
benefits: Supports healthy cardiac function and exercise tolerance
Supports normal blood pressure already within healthy range
safetyProfile: Very well tolerated. Commission E-approved. Rare reports of mild nausea or dizziness at high doses. Onset of effect may take 6–8 weeks of consistent use.
contraindications: Caution in patients already on prescription cardiac medications — additive effects possible
Do not use as primary treatment for diagnosed heart failure without physician oversight
drugInteractions: Digoxin — potential additive inotropic effect; monitor closely
Antihypertensive medications — additive blood pressure-lowering effect
Nitrates — potential additive vasodilation; monitor for hypotension
Phenylephrine — hawthorn may reduce vasopressor response
physicianNotes: Well-evidenced adjunct in mild-to-moderate cardiac support. Commission E and ESCOP approved for cardiac insufficiency NYHA class I-II. Strong safety data with long-term use. Monitor for additive effects in patients on antihypertensives or digoxin. Allow 6-8 weeks for full therapeutic effect.
patientGuidance: Hawthorn works gradually — allow 6 to 8 weeks before assessing full benefit. If you take heart medications or blood pressure medications, inform your physician before use.
